claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 4 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the eligibility rules for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex, who's 6, and Blake, who's 4.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have two children - Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 4).

Do both Alex and Blake live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes. Alex lives with me at home, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Alex lives with you at home in the UK, but Blake doesn't live with you.

Can you tell me where Blake lives and who Blake lives with?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with their other parent - my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not claiming Child Benefit for Blake at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Blake lives with your ex-partner but they're not claiming Child Benefit, I need to understand your involvement in Blake's care.

Do you make regular financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or towards their accommodation)? If so, approximately how much do you contribute?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with things like clothes and bits and pieces for Blake. I contribute about £20 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Just to clarify - are you and your ex-partner in agreement about who should claim Child Benefit for Blake, or would you both want to claim it?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We've agreed that I can claim it if I'm eligible.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you, that's helpful. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you or your partner (if you have one) earning over £60,000 per year individually? This doesn't affect eligibility, but there may be a tax charge to be aware of.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us earns over £60,000.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. One final question: are either Alex or Blake in hospital, in care, or receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in hospital or care, and they don't receive any benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.**

Here's a summary of your eligibility:

**For Alex (age 6):**
You are eligible because Alex lives with you in the UK and is under 16 years old. You are clearly responsible for Alex as they live in your home.

**For Blake (age 4):**
You are also eligible for Blake. Even though Blake lives with your ex-partner, you are considered responsible for Blake because you contribute £20 per week towards their upkeep (clothes and other items). This meets the requirement of paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since your ex-partner is not claiming Child Benefit and you've both agreed you can claim it, you meet all the criteria.

**Important points to note:**
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, so it's good that you and your ex-partner have agreed
- Since neither you nor your partner earns over £60,000, you won't have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge
- If your circumstances change (such as Blake moving back in with you, or changes to your contributions), you should inform HMRC

You can now proceed to make a claim for Child Benefit for both children. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Alex meets all these criteria, so you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 4 years old (under 16) and you live in the UK. Although Blake lives with their other parent (your ex-partner), you are contributing £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep. According to the eligibility rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money. The current Child Benefit rate for a child is approximately £25 per week for the first child and £16.95 per week for additional children. Your contribution of £20 per week meets the threshold for an additional child. Additionally, your ex-partner is not claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you have both agreed that you can claim it. Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and you meet the responsibility test through your financial contributions. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (6) and Blake (4). Alex lives with the claimant in the UK, making them clearly eligible under the standard rules. Blake lives with the claimant's ex-partner, but the claimant contributes £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep (clothes and other items), which meets the threshold for being considered responsible for the child. The ex-partner is not claiming Child Benefit and they have agreed the claimant can claim. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children.
